title: Mapped: Inside Carbon Brief’s Cosmos database of 1.8 million climate studies
gist: A short visual companion to Project Cosmos mapping its 1.8m climate studies as a 'cosmos' of citation clusters, highlighting that the Stern Review is the single most-cited publication across all IPCC reports.

insights.claim: Carbon Brief's Cosmos database of 1.8m climate studies is visualised as a 'cosmos' where citation clusters resolve into a physical-sciences (blue) and a social-sciences (yellow) macro-region, with finer 'galaxy' clusters such as medical studies cited by climate research.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Tom Pearson
insights.so_what: Illustrates the interdisciplinary structure of climate science — physical and social science as two distinct citation poles.
insights.quote: studies centred around the “physical sciences” (blue) and “social sciences” (yellow).

insights.claim: The Stern Review, the 700-page 2006 report by economist Nicholas Stern commissioned by the UK government on global warming's economic effects, is the most-cited publication across all IPCC reports.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Tom Pearson
insights.so_what: Confirms climate economics (not physical science) as the single most-referenced IPCC input — underscoring the policy weight of economic framing.
insights.quote: it is the most-cited publication across all the reports ever published by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC).
